Mojave Nature by Kurt Buzard


Cryptantha utahensis

Scented Forget-Me-Not

A sweet-smelling desert annual with clusters of white flowers that provide a rare, pleasant fragrance to the Mojave's spring landscape. Cryptantha utahensis stands out among the many popcorn flowers of the desert due to its noticeably fragrant blossoms. While many species in this genus require a lens to distinguish between nutlets, this species is easily identified in the field by its larger, fragrant flowers and its preference for well-drained, often calcareous slopes.

Learn More Details About This Plant

Plant Data

Names and Taxonomy

  • Common Names: scented forget-me-notutah catseye
  • Synonyms: krynitzkia utahensis
  • Family: boraginaceae
  • Genus Information: CA 87, NV 55, UT 48, AZ 41, NM 16, TX 11

Habit and Size

  • Duration: annual herb
  • Habit: erect
  • Shape: slender / branched
  • Size: 10–40 cm tall
  • Stem: covered in fine, appressed hairs (strigose)

Leaves

  • Color: gray-green
  • Shape: linear to narrowly oblanceolate
  • Edge: entire
  • Arrangement: alternate
  • Leaf Retention: annual

Flowers and Fruit

  • Sex: hermaphroditic
  • Flower Color: white with a yellow "eye" (throat)
  • Flower Size: 3–8 mm wide
  • Number of Petals: 5
  • Bloom: March–June
  • Fruit: 1 smooth, shiny nutlet

Habitat and Growing Conditions

  • Water Use: low
  • Light: full sun
  • Soil: sandy, gravelly, or rocky limestone soils
  • Habitat: creosote bush scrub, pinyon-juniper woodland

Distribution

  • Distribution: CA, NV, AZ, UT
  • Seen:
  • Elevation: 1000–7000 feet (300–2135 meters)

Gallery

Cryptantha utahensis

Noted for its distinct, honey-like fragrance during peak bloom.
